visualGoal: Make a maker feel that a real analog ship's helm is a cheap, achievable afternoon project — proof of the actual board(s) they'll wire, plus clear diagrams for the wiring and the board-choice decision, without forcing product cards on a low-density how-to. Real product proof where verified; instructional infographics fill the rest.

realProductPhotosNeeded.product: Leo Bodnar BU0836A USB Joystick Interface (pin-header / solder version)
realProductPhotosNeeded.notes: This is the exact board the official Bridge Command guide names, and it is the article's primary recommendation for cost — but its photo is NOT in the verified set, so it appears as text + links only. Sourcing a real photo would let it become a gearpick/shotgrid and is the top visual gap. Certification blocker if the piece must picture its primary recommended board.

realProductPhotosNeeded.product: 3x 10k linear-taper potentiometers (B10K rotary), the throttle/wheel pots
realProductPhotosNeeded.notes: Generic component, not a branded product. A clean trio shot (or the B10K printed code close-up) would reinforce the linear-vs-log point better than a stock render. Text-only for now; NOT embedded.

editorialCompositePrompts.prompt: Realistic maker workbench at warm workshop light: a part-built wooden ship's-helm panel with two throttle levers and a small wheel, exposed hookup wiring routed to an open slot left blank for a real interface-board product overlay, a loop of string wrapped around one pot shaft and anchored to a bracket, solder-free tidy build, marine-sim vibe, 16:9 editorial cover. No fake branded boards, no fake UI, no warped hardware.
editorialCompositePrompts.negativePrompt: fake logos, fabricated circuit board, impossible wiring, unreadable tiny text, procedural gradient art, force-feedback motors

infographicPrompts.title: How a potentiometer wires to the BU0836
content: Wire each pot's 3 pins to one 3-pin set on the board
Repeat x3: port throttle, starboard throttle, wheel
Optional: push-to-make horn button to a ground pin + column-1 pin
infographicPrompts.style: IgnitionSim command-center schematic, large readable labels, marine teal/gold, clean callout arrows on a stylized board footprint

infographicPrompts.title: Which DIY board should you buy?
content: Comfortable soldering + save money -> BU0836A (~$42, the guide's board)
Want custom logic / detents / encoders -> Arduino Leonardo (~$25 and down)
infographicPrompts.style: Numbered decision-tree ladder with product-photo placeholders for Codex overlay, marine palette

infographicPrompts.title: The string-friction trick
content: Bare pot = lever flops to idle
Wrap string around the pot shaft
Anchor it solidly to something rigid
Drag holds the lever where you set it
Alt: O-ring or felt washer under the knob
infographicPrompts.style: Simple 4-step before/after diagram, macro of a pot shaft with string wrap, marine palette, high readability

curatorScenePrompts.prompt: Realistic adult marine-sim curator Gus Calder at a tidy home workbench, one hand steadying a DIY throttle lever mid-build, calm hands-on maker energy, a soldering iron and a spool of string on the bench, product-overlay space beside him, practical not fantasy, 4:5 portrait.

screenshotsOrGameMediaNeeded.item: Bridge Command joystick-support / axis-mapping settings screen
screenshotsOrGameMediaNeeded.notes: A real screenshot of enabling joystick support and assigning the throttle/wheel axes would concretely back build step 7. Optional; not embedded. Verify licensing before use.

openQuestions: Source a verified BU0836A product photo so the article's primary (cheapest) recommended board is pictured, not just linked?
Confirm current Arduino Leonardo 2026 street price (body says ~$25 check-current)?
Confirm live USD for BU0836A (~$42) and BU0836X (~$77) from Leo Bodnar direct vs. Ricmotech before certifying?
Do we want a real 3x B10K pot-trio photo, or is the wiring infographic enough for the component visual?
